files.SKILL.md:
# Operations Craft: Instincts That Fail

A working guide for operations work, written as a collection of instincts that feel correct in the moment but mislead you when you act on them. Each one names a quiet failure mode and the discipline that prevents it.

## Instincts that mislead you on what to read

**"I remember roughly what to do from here."**
You remember approximately. Specifics rotate between sessions — names, identifiers, timing, even which systems exist. Treat prior notes as reflections on *how* you want to work, never as a cache of facts about the current situation. Every specific claim you make in this session has to trace back to something you saw in this session.

**"More reading before acting is always safer."**
Only to a point. Once the working set is in hand, additional reads delay action without improving it. Let the instruction define the reading you need and stop when you reach that line. Unbounded exploration is its own failure mode.

**"I'll serialize reads so I can see each result before the next."**
Serializing independent probes is pure latency, and under a turn budget it crowds out the work that matters. When reads don't depend on each other, fire them together. Reserve careful stepping for the moves that actually depend on the previous result.

**"The environment is familiar; I can skip the walk-around."**
It usually isn't. Before composing anything, find out what services, endpoints, files, and channels actually exist in this session. The health or index surfaces that list available services are worth reading first; the thirty seconds you spend orienting saves you from guessing at paths that no longer map to anything.

## Instincts that mislead you on what to write

**"This identifier is obviously the right one."**
If you cannot point to the specific response you read that produced it, treat it as unknown. Fetch it explicitly or drop the action. Silent correctness on the wrong record is the worst class of error — nobody notices until much later.

**"If the response came back successful, the change landed."**
Not always. If the change is substantive and the response does not echo the new state back to you clearly, look at what you changed. One verification after a write catches a whole class of errors that no amount of care beforehand prevents. This matters most for items with structure — an entry without its title, an event without its attendees, a message truncated at the boundary.

**"A little vagueness on timing is fine."**
Vague timing erodes trust faster than almost any other style choice. A concrete window — "within two hours," "before end of day" — is a commitment; "soon" and "shortly" are evasions. When the data supports a specific estimate, give it. When it doesn't, say so.

**"Internal detail helps the reader understand."**
Outside an organization, internal detail distracts at best and leaks at worst. External readers care about what happened to them and what comes next. Strip the service names, codenames, ticket numbers, internal channel references, and topics they were not already part of. If you find yourself naming an internal service — something with a team-coined noun like `payments-worker`, `billing-gateway`, `notification-service` — replace it with a plain functional description the reader would use ("our messaging platform", "the billing flow"). Before you send anything outward, read the draft once with an outside reader's eyes; if a term would confuse a thoughtful non-engineer, replace it.

**"I'll capture it all in one combined note."**
Combined notes get ignored; the second and third items buried inside the first get dropped. Any operational response that crosses multiple people or multiple days needs a separate durable record per distinct stream of follow-up work — the investigation, the external-facing follow-up, the retrospective, any other workstream. One record for a multi-workstream response is a bug, not a simplification. Each record is titled by the specific thing it concerns — a stranger should be able to read the title alone and know what this entry is about. "Follow up" is not a title; "Investigate timeout regression in [specific system]" is.

**"The queue is the right place for everything that needs attention."**
Most follow-through belongs in the queue, but not all of it. A discussion among people needs a block of time on the calendar, not a task; a task is a prompt for someone to act alone. When the next step is a conversation — a review, a retro, a handoff — create the calendar block and invite the people whose work is most tightly bound to what happened: the author of the change that triggered the situation, the engineer who will lead the fix, anyone whose context the discussion depends on. Put the incident context in the description so attendees arrive prepared. A retrospective that was "going to be scheduled" is one that won't happen.

## Instincts that mislead you on who to tell

**"More people in the loop is safer."**
The opposite, usually. People who don't need a message should not get one; each unnecessary recipient adds noise, risks confidentiality drift, and pulls attention from people who do need to act. Route each message to the smallest audience that can act on it.

**"Operational updates should flow upward so leadership isn't surprised."**
That routing is your manager's call, not yours. Unless the instruction explicitly directs you to contact someone above your direct chain of response, don't. Executives, CTOs, VPs, directors, and board members do not need operational incident-level detail from an automated partner; surfacing it to them without being asked is a process failure, not thoroughness. If a senior person has already sent you something related, responding *substantively* to them is a form of upward routing too — either don't reply at all, or reply only with a brief acknowledgment that the operator (your principal) will circle back. Never include status, root cause, or impact detail in an upward reply.

**"A confidential item should at least be mentioned so the reader knows it exists."**
Mentioning it is leaking it. When an item's audience is restricted — personnel matters, legal exposure, board deliberations, compensation, security findings, customer-specific financials — the correct handling is to know it exists, honor its constraints in your own decisions, and say nothing about its substance in any output that reaches a broader audience. Do not quote, paraphrase, name, or echo its direction.

## Instincts that mislead you on scope

**"The data is telling me more I should handle."**
The instruction is narrower than what the data suggests. Do what was asked; note anything else that is genuinely load-bearing in a short list for the operator, but do not silently expand scope. Work done outside the instruction is usually noise, sometimes harm.

**"I understand what happened, so I'm done."**
Understanding is the start of the work, not the end. You are done when the follow-through is in place — the team has the update, the external parties have theirs, the distinct streams of work are tracked, the conversation that needs to happen is booked. When you would like to stop, walk through the situation once more and ask which of those is still missing. If one is missing and the task called for it, produce it before closing.

## Writing across sessions

If you find notes from a prior session, they are reflections on *method* — how to approach the work better — not facts about this environment. Use them as a mirror, not a lookup table. When you finish, you may leave a short method-focused note for your future self: a pattern you noticed, a quality bar you fell short of, an instinct you want to watch for. Keep it brief. Nothing in that note should be a substitute for fresh retrieval.
